title: Workshop: AD-AS and Stagflation
summary: Group work on demand vs supply shocks using AD-AS. Compare 1973 oil, 2008 demand collapse, and 2020 pandemic via the chart presets.

questions.prompt: Define stagflation in one sentence. Which AD-AS shock produces it? Why is monetary policy in a bind during stagflation?
questions.notes: Stagflation = high P + low Y. Supply shock (SRAS left). Monetary policy fights one at the cost of worsening the other.

questions.prompt: The 'Demand boom' preset: who benefits in the short run? In the long run, where does SRAS go and why?
questions.notes: Short run: workers (jobs), firms (sales). Long run: Pe rises, SRAS shifts left, P stays elevated, Y returns to Yn.

questions.prompt: Compare 2008 to 2020 using the chart presets. Both are recessions, but the policy responses differed. Argue why the same response wouldn't have worked for both.
questions.notes: 2008: pure AD shock; fiscal stimulus is correct. 2020: AD + supply chain disruption; pure AD stimulus risked inflation (and did, by 2022).

questions.prompt: Draw the AD-AS path of the U.S. economy from 2019 through today. Label each year's (Y, P) point and identify the dominant shock.
questions.notes: 2019: baseline. 2020 Q2: AD/Yn both fall. 2021: AD recovers strongly. 2022: SRAS shifts left (supply). 2023-24: cooling AD.

questions.prompt: If the natural rate of output Yn falls (long-Covid effects, retirement, lower TFP), what happens to inflation at the old AD? Tie this to the 'natural rate of unemployment' concept.
questions.notes: Falling Yn at constant AD raises P. Equivalent to a higher natural rate of unemployment — the economy can't sustain prior output without overheating.

questions.prompt: Construct a policy scenario where AD-AS predicts no change in Y but a change in P. What's happening?
questions.notes: Equal-and-opposite AD shifts and SRAS shifts. E.g. AD right (stimulus) + SRAS left (supply shock) of matched magnitudes.
